data.title: The 10-Point: The Wall Street Journal's Guide to the Day's Top News
data.description:
By Emma Tucker

The United Arab Emirates is preparing to help reopen the Strait of Hormuz by force. Though Saudi Arabia and other Gulf states are turning against Iran's regime, the U.A.E. — which has been hit by more Iranian missiles and drones than even Israel — would be the first Persian Gulf country to become a combatant. The U.A.E. is also pushing the U.S. to occupy islands in the strategic waterway. Iran, meanwhile, continues to take aim at its Gulf neighbors, including the industries behind their pivot away from oil.

Today's Headlines

President Trump said the U.S. would leave Iran in two to three weeks, and plans to address the nation regarding the war later today. Follow our live coverage here.

Iran is targeting industries tied to Persian Gulf states' efforts to diversify beyond oil, including metals factories, bank offices and tourist sites.

Trump signed an executive order to create lists of people it considers eligible to vote in federal elections, as well as placing new restrictions on mail-in voting, moves that will likely face legal challenges.

Airlines are raising fares, adding baggage fees and dialing back routes as they try to cover the skyrocketing costs of jet fuel.

Live From The Markets

With the economy on the edge, here's a look at best-case and worst-case scenarios.

Gas has hit $4 a gallon. Is that really as bad as it sounds?

Stephen Nesbitt's Cliffwater helped bring private credit to the masses, but now the firm is racing to calm investors after steep withdrawals.

Read It Here First

The U.A.E. wants to join the fight to reopen Hormuz.

The U.A.E. is actively lobbying for a U.N. Security Council resolution that would authorize action to reopen the Strait of Hormuz by force, and has also said that the U.S. should occupy islands in the strait, including one that the U.A.E. claims. The Gulf nation's newly combative approach marks a fundamental shift in its strategic outlook, report Summer Said, David S. Cloud and Michael Amon. Iran, meanwhile, has been stepping up its bombardments of the Emirates, raining down more missiles and drones on the U.A.E. than on any other country.

America now has an EV Rust Belt, and high gas prices won't rescue it.

Magna International built a large factory in Michigan after securing a big contract to make parts for General Motors' new electric pickup. Five years later, that million-square-foot plant is mostly empty and losing money — a casualty of America's messy breakup with EVs. It's one of dozens of now desolate or sparsely used EV-parts plants across the country. Now the war in Iran has driven gas prices up so sharply that some are daring to wonder whether U.S. buyers might give EVs another look. But amid ongoing uncertainty, Magna and its big Detroit customers are forging ahead with plans to roll back EV investments, reports Sharon Terlep.

Expert Take

Q: It was a rough quarter for markets. What stocks and sectors succeeded amid the turmoil?

The quarter began with one jolt to the oil market — the capture of Venezuelan leader NicolÃ¡s Maduro — only to end with one far larger: the war with Iran. Reporter Jared Mitovich wrote about which investments outperformed the broader market.

A: In a period marked by Middle East conflict and surging oil prices, it may come as no surprise that energy stocks were among the biggest winners in a forgettable first quarter. But America's oil-and-gas giants such as Exxon Mobil and ConocoPhillips weren't the only early 2026 successes. Here are a few others:

Agriculture shares and commodities: The closure of Hormuz has benefited American fertilizer producers, which investors are betting will gain market share while competitors in the Middle East struggle to get their output to market.

Defense companies: An increasing U.S. appetite for foreign intervention from Venezuela to Iran — and Trump's request for a $1.5 trillion defense budget-tipped financial markets in weapon makers' favor in the first quarter.

The U.S. dollar: The greenback extended its winning streak, helped by the U.S.'s position as a net oil exporter. It could rise further due to the energy price shock, Morgan Stanley strategists have said.

Tariff-exposed companies: These businesses, such as construction firms and appliance makers, climbed after the Supreme Court struck down many of the duties.

See The Story

These are the highs and lows of a $939 upgrade to business class on United Airlines.

WSJ columnist Dawn Gilbertson took advantage of a tantalizing offer to upgrade from economy to a plush Polaris business-class cabin. She enjoyed the champagne, ice-cream-sundae cart and Saks Fifth Avenue bedding. But the largely inoperable Wi-Fi was a huge downer — underscoring the harsh reality that inconsistency still rules in the airline arms race for big-spending travelers.

Happening Today

The Supreme Court will hear oral arguments on Trump's executive order seeking to end birthright citizenship.

NASA's Artemis II mission is set to embark on the deepest human spaceflight since the final Apollo moon mission in 1972, a big test for the agency and several contractors.

Earnings: Cal-Maine Foods, Conagra Brands, Lamb Weston Holdings

Number Of The Day:

22

The number of corporate deals valued at $10 billion or more that have been announced globally so far in 2026 — a record quarterly number, according to LSEG data. This week alone, Unilever unveiled a more than $65 billion deal, including debt, to combine its food business with spice maker McCormick, and Sysco agreed to buy Jetro Restaurant Depot for over $29 billion, including debt.

And Finally...

How did these five friends become the kings of 'Seinfeld' trivia?

Mike Rapanaro and four of his friends in the New York City area make up the Venetian Blinds, a team that's dominating the "Seinfeld" trivia circuit. They take inspiration from the characters in their daily lives — and refuse to watch any other TV shows. One of them falls asleep to episodes hoping some details stick in his head. Another listens to the show's audio while showering.

data.title: ZAWYA: IBPC Dubai AGM reinforces Indo-UAE economic partnership
data.description:
Dubai, United Arab Emirates: The Indian Business and Professional Council (IBPC Dubai) convened its Annual General Meeting (AGM) this weekend, underscoring its pivotal role as a catalyst for Indo-UAE collaboration and reaffirming its commitment to strengthening bilateral economic ties in alignment with long-term strategic visions.

In his opening address, Chairman Siddharth Balachandran emphasized the enduring UAE-India brotherhood, noting that IBPC Dubai’s purpose and relevance remain central to advancing shared prosperity. He reiterated the Council’s transformation journey, positioning IBPC Dubai as a unifying institutional platform that continues to empower the Indian business community in the UAE through collaboration, resilience, and a shared forward-looking vision.

IBPC Dubai reported a year of exceptional growth and engagement, marked by a significant expansion in its membership base and a strong shift toward inclusivity, particularly with increased female participation. The Council substantially scaled its events, outreach, and digital presence, while launching new initiatives such as the IBPC Dubai Inspire Podcast to enhance member value. This progress was complemented by robust financial performance and a strengthened balance sheet, reinforcing institutional stability and future readiness.

The AGM also strongly reinforced IBPC Dubai’s commitment to sectoral strengthening and realignment and growth of its Focus Groups, ensuring sharper advocacy and deeper engagement in line with the UAE’s economic agenda and India’s vision of Viksit Bharat (Developed India). This alignment supports Dubai’s ambitious D33 Agenda, which aims to double the emirate’s economy by 2033 through over 100 transformative projects spanning innovation, infrastructure, and sustainable growth.

Across all Focus Groups, enhanced emphasis will be placed on institutional reach, solar energy, sustainability, and Artificial Intelligence, reflecting emerging priorities shaping the future of business and economic cooperation.

Reaffirming its commitment to transparent and democratic governance, the Council highlighted the integrity of its electoral process. Outgoing Election Officer Skandan Mahalingam oversaw a credible, fair, and transparent election for Focus Group Convenors, reinforcing member trust and institutional credibility while strengthening participatory leadership.

Dr. Sahitya Chaturvedi was confirmed for a second tenure as Secretary General, ensuring continuity, stability, and sustained strategic execution.

Focus Group Convenors were elected to lead key sectors critical to Indo-UAE collaboration, including Health, Education, Wellness and Pharma led by Sreekumar Brahmanandan of Dubai Investment Industries, Financial Services and Capital Markets by Sunny Narang of HSBC, Tech, Digital and Innovation by Karthik Raman of RevDau, Professional and Business Services by James Mathew of UHY James, Real Estate and Built Environment by Rajiv Neelivethil of ESPA, Energy and Climate by Advait Thakur, Hospitality and Tourism by Amit Vardhan of Prism Advertising, bringing together deep industry expertise across priority domains.

Looking ahead, IBPC Dubai is gearing up to host two flagship platforms: the West Asia Business Conclave in April 2026 and the Family Business Conference in May 2026. These initiatives are expected to further strengthen cross-border partnerships, thought leadership, and institutional engagement across the region.

Reflecting the Council’s broader vision, Dr. Chaturvedi stated, “Institutions that endure are those that evolve with purpose. IBPC Dubai stands committed to shaping narratives of trust, transparency, and transformation, bridging economies, empowering enterprises, and contributing meaningfully to the shared journeys of D33 and Viksit Bharat.”

Send us your press releases to pressrelease.zawya@lseg.com

data.title: Cal-Maine Foods 3Q Sales Sink on Lower Egg Prices
data.description:
By Connor Hart

Cal-Maine Foods logged lower profit and sales in its fiscal third quarter, hurt by materially lower egg prices compared with historic levels seen in the prior year.

Still, the declines weren't as steep as Wall Street had expected, as Cal-Maine continues to diversify its sales mix by expanding the scale and geographic reach of its egg business and investing to support the growth of our prepared foods business.

The egg producer on Wednesday posted a profit of $50.5 million for its quarter ended Feb. 28, compared with $508.5 million in last year's comparable quarter. Quarterly earnings of $1.06 a share topped the 70 cents a share that analysts polled by FactSet had expected.

Sales fell 53% to $667 million but came in ahead of the $642.5 million that Wall Street had modeled.

Conventional egg sales decreased 72%, hurt by lower selling prices and sales volumes. Sales of specialty eggs, which include free-range, pasture-raised and other premium varieties, were down 12%, as lower selling prices were partially offset by higher volumes.

Sales of prepared foods jumped to $63.6 million from $11.8 million a year ago, largely due to Cal-Maine's previous acquisition of Echo Lake, a breakfast-foods manufacturer. The company expects the unit to continue to grow as additional capacity comes online and utilization improves.

"By building on the structural strength of our core shell egg platform while expanding specialty eggs, egg products and prepared foods, we are enhancing the resilience and diversification of our business," Chief Executive Sherman Miller said.

Write to Connor Hart at connor.hart@wsj.com

data.title: Copper touches two-week high on hopes for quick end to Iran war
data.description:
By Eric Onstad

Copper prices extended gains on Wednesday to hit their highest in two weeks and aluminium fell on hopes that the Iran war is close to ending.

Benchmark three-month copper on the London Metal Exchange was up 0.6% at $12,408 a metric ton by 1000 GMT after hitting $12,492.50, the highest since March 18.

That marked its fourth straight session of gains, but copper was still well below a record high of $14,527.50 hit on January 29.

"The market wants to believe that we're getting closer to an end to this escalation, even though we still have economic clouds hanging over the markets, which are quite grey and could worsen," said Ole Hansen, head of commodity strategy at Saxo Bank in Copenhagen.

Copper joined stocks and other financial markets in rallying after President Donald Trump said the end of the war on Iran could be near.

The most-active copper contract on the Shanghai Futures Exchange gained 1.5% to 97,030 yuan ($14,093.57) a ton, following a rise to 97,250 yuan - the highest since March 19.

Metals also got a boost from data on Wednesday showing the private manufacturing sector in top metals consumer China expanded in March for a fourth straight month.

That came after data from an official survey on Tuesday showed activity grew at the fastest pace in a year.

Higher premiums and falling Chinese inventories are showing physical copper demand is improving.

Stocks monitored by SHFE (CU-STX-SGH) declined for a second consecutive week to 359,135 tons as of March 27.

"That indicates there was some pent-up demand and that the lower prices that we reached earlier this month did trigger some buying," Hansen added.

Metals gains were also buoyed by a weaker U.S. dollar , making greenback-backed commodities more affordable for investors using other currencies.

LME aluminium (CAL3) fell 0.5% to $3,451 a ton after hefty gains in recent days due to supply fears after Iranian strikes damaged key Gulf smelters over the weekend.

Among other metals, LME zinc added 0.2% to $3,231.50 a ton, lead gained 1% to $1,922, nickel rose 0.5% to $17,190 and tin climbed 1.1% to $47,280.

($1 = 6.8847 Chinese yuan renminbi)

data.title: Oil Wavers as Uncertainty Over War Remains — WSJ
data.description:
By Giulia Petroni and Angus Berwick

Oil prices fell overnight, with the global benchmark Brent crude briefly slipping below $100 a barrel after President Trump signaled he wants to wind down the war in Iran soon.

Later on Wednesday, though, Brent crude clawed back its losses to trade close to $104, as Iran cast shade on Trump's optimism and uncertainty remained over when energy flows in the Persian Gulf could return to normal.

Here's what analysts are saying:

  • "The market is caught between the largest supply disruption in modern history and growing de-escalation rhetoric from Washington," said Neil Crosby, associate vice president of oil analytics at Sparta Commodities. "Even if a ceasefire materialises, restoring supply takes weeks to months-damaged facilities need repair, the cargo backlog is enormous," he said, adding that the Strait of Hormuz reopening is far from guaranteed.
  • While crude benchmarks eased overnight, the physical market better reflects the supply shock. Analysts at Saxo Bank said buyers of Brent cargoes were still paying a premium of around $9 a barrel above the front-month futures price, which underscored "ongoing supply stress."
  • Analysts at Norwegian investment bank DNB Carnegie estimated that total oil flows through Hormuz are at about a quarter of their pre-war levels, taking into account the re-routing of some flows via Saudi Arabi's Red Sea port. "Consequently, we remain at extremely high disruption levels of oil flow from the Middle East," they said in a note.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Euro Bank Bonds Supply Declines in March Given Middle East Conflict — Market Talk
data.description: Issuance of new euro bank bonds slowed in March as debt issuers stayed cautious due to the Middle East war, ING's Marine Leleux says in a note. New bank bond supply amounted to 17.3 billion euros in March ($20 billion), about half of the total new supply in March 2025 and a third of February's level, Leleux says. The largest share of new supply was in covered bonds in March, she says. Covered bonds are debt securities issued by financial institutions and are backed by a pool of assets such as mortgages or public loans. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

data.title: ZAWYA: Standard Chartered Foundation and Village Capital launch Women in Tech 7 in Bahrain
data.description:
Bahrain – Standard Chartered Foundation, in partnership with Village Capital and Bahrain FinTech Bay, today announced the launch of the seventh cohort of the Women in Tech Bahrain Accelerator, reaffirming its long-term commitment to Bahrain’s innovation economy and confidence in the Kingdom’s future.

At a time of continued geopolitical and economic uncertainty, this initiative underscores Standard Chartered’s commitment to Bahrain, building on its track record, with a focus on supporting entrepreneurship, innovation and national talent.

The initiative is part of the Standard Chartered Foundation’s global programme, which supports women-led tech startups through tailored training, catalytic funding, and access to world-class networks. Bahrain FinTech Bay will deliver the programme locally, leveraging its strong track record in entrepreneur support and ecosystem development.

Through the initiative, selected startups will have access to up to BHD 28,000 (USD 75,000) in equity-free funding from the Standard Chartered Foundation, along with three months’ residence at Bahrain FinTech Bay. Since its launch, the Women in Tech accelerator in Bahrain has supported 61 startups through mentorship, with 22 receiving a combined BHD 145,367 (USD 385,000) in funding to help scale their ventures.

Dr. Boutros Klink, Chief Executive Officer & Head of Coverage, Bahrain, Standard Chartered, said: “Launching the seventh cohort of Women in Tech Bahrain is a strong expression of our confidence in Bahrain, its future, and the opportunities being created across its innovation economy. At a time when global conditions remain challenging, we believe it is more important than ever to back entrepreneurship, support national talent, and invest in initiatives that can help build long-term economic resilience.”

He added “For more than a century, Standard Chartered has stood alongside Bahrain’s progress. That commitment is unwavering. Through partnerships like this, we continue to invest in entrepreneurship, strengthen the innovation ecosystem, and support sustainable growth for the Kingdom.”

With women entrepreneurs continuing to face systemic barriers to scaling their businesses, the initiative is designed to help them overcome those challenges. Through the accelerator, women founders will benefit from tailored investment-readiness support, grant funding, and mentorship opportunities that will enable their ventures to grow sustainably and create long-lasting community impact.

Nakami Walunywa, Regional Director, Africa and Middle East at Village Capital said: “In 2025, 71 women-led startups in the programme grew their businesses and collectively generated over USD 2 million in additional revenue. This demonstrates that when founders have access to structured, locally-embedded support and catalytic funding, they can strengthen their strategies, engage investors confidently, and unlock sustainable impact. In 2026, we’re continuing to create the conditions for even more women-led startups to thrive and positively impact their communities.”

From his end, Bader Sater, CEO of Bahrain FinTech Bay, under which Innovate for Bahrain operates, commented: "Reaching the seventh cohort of this programme is something we are genuinely proud of. Seven cohorts in, and the ambition we see from Bahraini women in tech has only grown stronger. Year after year, we see founders walk in with bold ideas and leave ready to compete, and that is exactly what this initiative set out to do. This programme was built on the belief that the right support at the right time can change a startup's trajectory, and we have seen that proven time and again. We are glad to be delivering this once again through Innovate for Bahrain, in partnership with Standard Chartered and our strategic partners, and we cannot wait to see the ideas, the energy, and the stories this cohort will bring.”

Women startup founders from Bahrain are invited to apply, with applications closing on 6 June 2026. Successful applicants will participate in the Accelerator between June and August 2026.

Further information

Media contact

Noora Al Nusuf

Head, Corporate Affairs and Brand & Marketing, Bahrain and

Islamic Banking Communications

Standard Chartered Bank

Phone: +973 17150760

Email:   Noora.alnusuf@sc.com

About Standard Chartered 

We are a leading international banking group, with a presence in 53 of the world’s most dynamic markets. Our purpose is to drive commerce and prosperity through our unique diversity, and our heritage and values are expressed in our brand promise, here for good.

Standard Chartered PLC is listed on the London and Hong Kong stock exchanges. For more stories and expert opinions please visit https://www.sc.com/en/insights/Insights at sc.com. Follow Standard Chartered on X, LinkedIn, Instagram and Facebook.

About Standard Chartered Foundation    

The Standard Chartered Foundation is a charitable organisation established by Standard Chartered in 2019. It is committed to helping tackle inequality by empowering under-served young people, especially women and people with disabilities. It sets the strategy for - and funds - youth employability and entrepreneurship programmes across Standard Chartered’s markets, helping young people access the skills, networks and support they need to secure decent work and grow their businesses. The Standard Chartered Foundation is a registered charity in England and Wales (charity number 1184946). See the website for more information: https://www.sc.com/en/about/investing-in-communities/scfoundation/Standard Chartered Foundation.

About Village Capital

Village Capital is a global leader in impact investing, helping early-stage startups access the type of social and financial capital they need to grow – reshaping the supply and demand sides of capital flows as a result.

Since 2009, Village Capital has directly supported more than 2,100 impact startups in 70 countries. Together, these founders have raised over $9.6B across economic mobility, environmental sustainability, and access to essential services. It has made 116 investments through its various affiliated funds, including Vilcap Investments, which has invested in 91 peer-selected companies. Additionally, 26,000 entrepreneurs have used its Abaca web app to take at least one assessment, helping them expand their access to financial capital.

Learn more at www.vilcap.com and follow @villagecapital.

About Bahrain FinTech Bay (BFB):

Bahrain FinTech Bay (BFB) is the leading fintech hub in the Kingdom of Bahrain and the region's gateway for financial technology innovation. We incubate impactful and scalable fintech initiatives through innovation labs, acceleration programs, curated activities, and educational programs. Our mandate is to attract international fintech players to establish a presence in Bahrain, support the local ecosystem by driving sustainable growth within the sector, and cultivate talent through education and the adoption of essential finance and technology skills across the MENA region.

Bahrain FinTech Bay is a subsidiary of The BENEFIT Company.

data.title: Palm Oil Ends Lower on Likely Profit-Taking — Market Talk
data.description: Palm oil futures closed lower, with the Bursa Malaysia Derivatives contract for June delivery falling 60 ringgit to 4,768 ringgit a metric ton. The futures likely fell on profit-taking activities after a recent rally, Kenanga Futures analysts say in a note. This is despite overnight gains in rival soy oils and continued optimism surrounding Indonesia's advancing biofuel mandate, they add. The analysts peg the support levels for the June contract at 4,780 ringgit and resistance levels at 4,950 ringgit. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

data.title: Synergy CHC 2025 10-K: Revenue $30.4M, Net Loss $(12.34)M
data.description:
Synergy CHC reported fiscal 2025 revenue of $30.38 million and a net loss of $12.34 million, a swing from net income of $2.12 million in 2024 driven by lower sales, higher expenses, and receivable write-offs. Gross profit was $20.30 million, or 67% of revenue, modestly down versus the prior year. The results reflect reduced nutraceutical and beverage-related sales alongside operational and licensing adjustments during the year.

Financial Highlights

  • Revenue: $30.38 million (declined from $34.83 million in 2024).
  • Gross Profit: $20.30 million (67% of revenue; down from $23.64 million, 68% in 2024).
  • Net Income: $(12.34) million, compared with $2.12 million in 2024 (turn to loss due to lower revenue, higher expenses, and receivable write-offs).

Business Highlights

  • Revenue Trend: Reported revenue declined approximately 13% year-over-year to $30.4 million in 2025, driven primarily by lower nutraceutical sales and reduced beverage testing activity.
  • Channel & Product Shift: Management shifted focus toward developing beverage SKUs and reduced emphasis on core nutraceuticals during 2025.
  • Brand & IP Activity: Core brands FOCUSfactor and Flat Tummy remained central to the business; the company engaged in IP licensing activity but subsequently canceled and reversed a $2.9 million license.
  • Product Launches & Market Development: Launched a ready-to-drink FOCUSfactor beverage and expanded market development efforts around beverage offerings.
  • Workforce Actions: Implemented cost reductions by laying off 13 employees as part of rightsizing efforts.

Original SEC Filing: Synergy CHC Corp. [ SNYR ] - 10-K - Mar. 31, 2026

data.title: Osprey Bitcoin Trust 2025 10-K: Net loss $13.45M, NAV decline; EPS impact reflected in trust operations
data.description:
Osprey Bitcoin Trust reported a net decrease in net assets resulting from operations of $13.45 million for the year ended December 31, 2025, driven primarily by losses on its Bitcoin holdings and redemptions that reduced managed assets. The trust recorded a net realized and unrealized loss on its investment in Bitcoin of $11.32 million (comprised of $31.71 million realized gain and $43.02 million unrealized depreciation) and incurred a management fee of $0.95 million paid in USD. These results contributed to a 24% reduction in net assets to $136.7 million during 2025.

Financial Highlights

  • Net income / Net decrease in net assets from operations: $(13.45) million for the year ended Dec 31, 2025.
  • Net realized and unrealized gain (loss) on investment in Bitcoin: $(11.32) million for 2025, composed of a $31.71 million realized gain and $43.02 million unrealized depreciation.
  • Management fee: $0.95 million for the year ended Dec 31, 2025 (paid in USD and included in operations).
  • Net assets: Declined 24% in 2025 to $136.7 million (reflecting market movement in Bitcoin and redemptions).

Business Highlights

  • NAV and asset movement: Net assets fell 24% in 2025 to $136.7 million, driven by the decline in Bitcoin prices and approximately $30.6 million in investor redemptions.
  • Market sensitivity: Operating results and NAV are highly correlated with Bitcoin volatility; the trust experienced material swings in NAV and share pricing as a result.
  • Expense and fee management: The sponsor covers routine operating expenses; the management fee is paid in USD and funded via Bitcoin sales when necessary.
  • Market access and listing: Shares moved from OTCQX to a primary exchange listing on December 19, 2025, which narrowed the trust's historical premium/discount to NAV.
  • Liquidity mechanisms: The trust employs short-term Trade Credits to provide settlement flexibility; repayment of Trade Credits is required in the same asset type (BTC or cash).

Original SEC Filing: Osprey Bitcoin Trust [ OBTC ] - 10-K - Mar. 31, 2026

data.title: OLB Group 2025 10-K: Revenue $8.68M, EPS (1.74)
data.description:
OLB Group reported 2025 revenue of $8.68 million and a net loss per common share of $(1.74), with overall net loss improving versus the prior year largely due to lower operating expenses and reduced impairments. The company generated an operating loss of $(5.17) million and a net loss of $(5.87) million for the year, reflecting continued revenue pressure from its digital products and processing services. Management highlights operational actions including vendor transitions and a planned spin-off that are intended to improve margins and positioning going forward.

Financial Highlights

  • Total revenue: $8.68 million, decreased 32.4% versus 2024, driven primarily by lower Moola Cloud digital product and processing revenue.
  • Operating income (loss): $(5.17) million, an improvement from the prior year operating loss of $(11.68) million, driven by lower impairment, depreciation and general & administrative expenses.
  • Net loss: $(5.87) million, improved by $5.35 million versus 2024, principally from reduced operating expenses and impairments.
  • Net loss applicable to common stockholders: $(6.68) million, which includes a $775,000 deemed dividend on preferred stock.
  • Net loss per common share, basic and diluted: $(1.74); weighted average shares outstanding: 3.84 million.

Business Highlights

  • Revenue trend: Revenue declined 32% year-over-year to $8.68 million in 2025, attributed to lower transaction, subscription, and digital product sales within Moola Cloud.
  • Channel & vendor transition: The company is transitioning Moola Cloud vendors to improve pricing; the majority of vendor transitions are expected to be completed by Q1 2026.
  • Merchant processing scale: eVance processes more than $100 million and approximately 1.4 million transactions monthly; SecurePay is used by roughly 3,000 merchants, representing about $9 million gross merchandise volume per month.
  • Product and segment developments: Highlights include the 2019 launch of instant merchant/ISO onboarding and the formation of subsidiaries OLBit (money transmission) and DMINT (Bitcoin mining).
  • Operational actions and outlook: Management reduced amortization/depreciation and recorded asset impairments, leading to lower operating expenses; the company is pursuing a spin-off of DMINT pending S-1 filing and Nasdaq approval, which may affect future operational structure and focus.

Original SEC Filing: OLB GROUP, INC. [ OLB ] - 10-K - Mar. 31, 2026
